q: COPY INTO idempotency: how does it track which files have already been loaded, and when would you override it?
a: COPY INTO maintains internal metadata about every file it has successfully loaded into the target Delta table. On each subsequent run it skips any file already recorded, even if the file has been modified since it was loaded — the tracking is by file path, not by content hash. This makes it safe to put COPY INTO in a scheduled job without deduplication logic. The override is the FORCE option (COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'true')), which disables idempotency entirely and reloads every matching file regardless of prior loads. Use FORCE when you have corrected upstream files and need to reload them, or during a targeted historical backfill on a specific path subset. The FILES = () clause lets you load only named files on demand — a cleaner alternative to FORCE when you know exactly which files need reprocessing. The common mistake is running COPY INTO with FORCE on a broad path to fix one bad file and inadvertently duplicating all other data — always combine FORCE with a narrow PATTERN or FILES filter.
fj: I'd just re-run COPY INTO on the same path — it'll skip already-loaded files automatically.
fs: Skipping is correct for the happy path, but when you need to reload corrected files you must either use COPY_OPTIONS ('force' = 'true') with a narrow PATTERN or FILES list, or delete the specific rows from the target and re-run. Running FORCE without a filter reloads everything and duplicates all previously ingested data.

code.label: FORCE on full path — duplicates all prior data
lines: -- Danger: reloads ALL files, duplicating rows
